title: Down and out from Rabat to Cairo: The Iran war and North African instability – European Council on Foreign Relations
gist: The renewed US-Iran war and closure of the Strait of Hormuz are hammering North African import-dependent economies through energy, fertiliser and remittance shocks, deepening the Algeria-Morocco power imbalance and Libyan instability, while creating an opening for Europe to deploy Special Drawing Rights and its Mediterranean pact to buy influence cheaply.

insights.claim: After the US launched new strikes on Iran on July 8th 2026, the two countries resumed exchanging fire on Gulf assets, again constricting shipping through the Strait of Hormuz and pushing global fuel prices back up.
insights.speaker: Tarek Megerisi
insights.so_what: A reopened Hormuz risk premium is back in play for oil markets — North Africa is a leading-indicator region for the pain this transmits to import-dependent economies.
insights.locator: To nobody's surprise, the US and Iran are back at war

insights.claim: Tunisia built its budget on an assumed oil price of $63 per barrel, but prices soared past $100 during the first wave of the Iran war after Iran closed the Strait of Hormuz and struck Gulf energy terminals, nearly depleting Tunisia's emergency reserves within weeks and forcing it to rely on quiet energy loans from neighbours.
insights.speaker: Tarek Megerisi
insights.so_what: A ~60% overshoot versus budgeted oil price shows just how thin North African fiscal buffers are against Gulf-conflict shocks — a template for modeling second-round fiscal stress this round.
insights.locator: The Tunisian government, for example, already locked in a Sisyphean struggle

insights.claim: Egypt was forced to source gas from Libya and costly spot markets after Israel declared force majeure on key gas exports following the Gulf shutdown, and the government imposed rolling blackouts after 9pm in major cities.
insights.speaker: Tarek Megerisi
insights.so_what: Egypt's forced pivot to spot-market gas and blackouts is a concrete marker of energy-security fragility to watch as the war reignites.
insights.locator: Egypt, similarly, was forced to go to Libya

insights.claim: North Africa enters this renewed round of the Iran war with energy reserves already depleted and government coffers dry from the first wave, unlike at the initial outbreak of fighting.
insights.speaker: Tarek Megerisi
insights.so_what: Buffers that cushioned the first shock are gone, implying the second round of the war could produce faster, sharper fiscal and social stress in the region.
insights.locator: The region now faces this new round of fighting

insights.claim: Even North Africa's oil-producing states (Algeria and Libya) failed to capture the upside of high oil prices because gains were offset by loans extended to neighbours, production limits, and rising domestic energy demand; Libya's first unified budget in years was gravely unbalanced before it was even signed.
insights.speaker: Tarek Megerisi
insights.so_what: Don't assume regional oil exporters are net beneficiaries of the Hormuz risk premium — structural offsets erode the windfall.
insights.locator: North Africa's oil economies were also affected

insights.claim: Disrupted exports of urea and sulphur have crippled the fertiliser industry central to North African economies and food supply; Morocco hosts the world's largest phosphate exporter, Egypt is among the world's largest fertiliser consumers and exporters, and Tunisia's phosphate industry — though a core economic asset — is entirely dependent on imported inputs.
insights.speaker: Tarek Megerisi
insights.so_what: Fertiliser-supply-chain disruption from the Iran war is an underappreciated transmission channel into North African food security and export revenue, with Morocco's phosphate dominance a specific watch point.
insights.locator: It is not just energy. Exports of two seemingly innocuous chemicals

insights.claim: North Africa imports over half its food, and regional food insecurity had already doubled between 2019 and 2024, meaning falling export revenues and rising domestic agricultural costs from the war are acutely painful.
insights.speaker: Tarek Megerisi
insights.so_what: Pre-existing doubling of food insecurity (2019-2024) means the region has little slack to absorb a new food-cost shock — a driver of social unrest risk.
insights.locator: As a region that imports over half its food

themes.theme: Remittances and Gulf investment as the next shoe to drop

insights.claim: The author identifies remittances and Arab Gulf investment as North Africa's two largest foreign-currency streams, and argues the outlook for both is worse than for trade — the Gulf is the region's main labour market, with Egypt alone having 3.3 million nationals working there.
insights.speaker: Tarek Megerisi
insights.so_what: Remittances and Gulf FDI are the key forward-looking indicators to monitor for North African currency stress, more so than trade flows already stressed in the first wave.
insights.locator: For North Africa's two largest foreign currency streams

insights.claim: Morocco received roughly €11.4bn in remittances last year (about 8% of GDP), already running 10% higher this year; Tunisia received €2.6bn (5.6% of GDP, about 30% of its foreign-currency reserves); and Egypt's remittances grew 40.5% year-on-year to €36.2bn, roughly 12% of GDP.
insights.speaker: Tarek Megerisi
insights.so_what: Concrete country-level remittance dependency ratios (Egypt 12% of GDP, Tunisia 30% of FX reserves) quantify exactly how exposed each economy is to a Gulf labour-market shock.
insights.locator: Last year, the value of remittances to Morocco was around €11.4bn

insights.claim: The Gulf is North Africa's largest source of foreign investment — it has repeatedly bailed out Egypt, funded rare foreign projects in Tunisia's otherwise seized economy, and the UAE alone accounts for 19% of Morocco's foreign direct investment.
insights.speaker: Tarek Megerisi
insights.so_what: UAE's 19% share of Moroccan FDI is a specific concentration risk if Gulf economies retrench under war pressure.
insights.locator: The Gulf is also the largest source of foreign investment

insights.claim: There is a deep-seated fear among North African capitals that Gulf economies are being harder hit by the war than publicly disclosed, with anecdotal expectations of mass layoffs and cuts to foreign spending.
insights.speaker: Tarek Megerisi
insights.so_what: If true, official Gulf economic data may understate war damage — a signal to discount headline Gulf resilience narratives and watch for lagged layoff/investment-cut news.
insights.locator: Among North African capitals, there is a deep-seated fear

insights.claim: Poor finances have fuelled a long-running battle between Tunisia's government and protesters, while 2025 saw Morocco's worst protests since the 2011 Arab Spring, and the author expects cost-of-living and inequality issues to worsen further under the renewed war.
insights.speaker: Tarek Megerisi
insights.so_what: Both Tunisia and Morocco carry elevated near-term social-unrest risk tied directly to war-driven cost-of-living pressure.
insights.locator: Poor finances have fuelled a long-running battle

insights.claim: Algeria has leveraged high oil prices to accelerate its energy sector, consolidate a trilateral Maghreb union that pointedly excludes Morocco, court Europe with energy supply, and advance the trans-Saharan pipeline linking Nigerian and Nigerien oil to Algeria's export terminals — tilting the Algeria-Morocco balance of power decisively toward Algiers.
insights.speaker: Tarek Megerisi
insights.so_what: Algeria's strengthened hand versus Morocco, at a time Morocco's US/UAE/Israel allies are distracted, raises the probability of new flashpoints, notably over Western Sahara.
insights.locator: Amid it all, the rivalry between Algeria and Morocco has intensified

insights.claim: The tilted Algeria-Morocco balance of power, occurring while Morocco's closest allies (the US, UAE and Israel) are otherwise occupied with the Iran war, could heighten tensions and other flashpoints across the region, most notably over Western Sahara.
insights.speaker: Tarek Megerisi
insights.so_what: Western Sahara is flagged as the most likely flashpoint to watch if the Algeria-Morocco rivalry escalates amid distracted Western/Gulf allies.
insights.locator: This has toppled the regional balance of power

insights.claim: In Libya, high oil prices enticed Turkey and the US to use energy-sector development as bait for a new political agreement, but this has instead stoked mass protests from a population long-promised elections and enduring its worst cost-of-living crisis since the 1950s.
insights.speaker: Tarek Megerisi
insights.so_what: External energy-deal diplomacy in Libya is backfiring politically, raising instability risk rather than the intended stabilization.
insights.locator: In Libya, high oil prices enticed Turkey and the US

insights.claim: Anxieties over Gulf relations drove Egyptian president Abdel Fattah el-Sisi to send air-defence systems to the Gulf and visit Abu Dhabi to shore up ties, after the UAE expelled thousands of Pakistani labourers seemingly in retaliation for Islamabad's diplomatic efforts.
insights.speaker: Tarek Megerisi
insights.so_what: Egypt is actively spending scarce military/diplomatic capital to protect its Gulf relationship — a sign of how existentially important that relationship is to Cairo.
insights.locator: Similar anxieties drove Egyptian president Abdel Fattah el-Sisi

themes.theme: Europe's opening to deepen influence cheaply

insights.claim: The author argues North Africa's crisis is an opportunity for Europe to advance its Mediterranean-pact strategy of rebuilding southern relationships, since interested EU states like Spain, France and Italy could donate or cheaply loan their IMF Special Drawing Rights to help struggling regional economies weather the storm.
insights.speaker: Tarek Megerisi
insights.so_what: SDR reallocation to North Africa is a low-cost, high-leverage policy tool for the EU that investors in regional stability should watch for as a stabilization catalyst.
insights.locator: Interested countries, like Spain, France and Italy could donate

insights.claim: The author recommends the European Commission extend the food and resilience facility for the southern neighbourhood to help states cope with the fertiliser crisis, then build longer-term engagement via green power generation, private-sector investment facilitation, and Global Gateway infrastructure projects.
insights.speaker: Tarek Megerisi
insights.so_what: Global Gateway and green-energy infrastructure investment in North Africa is positioned as the follow-on opportunity for European capital once short-term stabilization measures are in place.
insights.locator: Alongside this they should encourage the European Commission

insights.claim: The author warns that if Europe fails to respond to North Africa's crisis, it will once again be the passive bystander — described as the worst geopolitical role possible at a moment of both great opportunity and dire crisis.
insights.speaker: Tarek Megerisi
insights.so_what: Frames European inaction itself as a strategic cost, not a neutral default — a call to action for EU policymakers and a risk factor if they don't act.
insights.quote: if Europe refuses to respond it will once again be assuming the position of the passive bystander
insights.locator: After a year of unrest, North Africa may very well stabilise itself
